meta.title: Kiss All The Time, Disco Occasionally: An Album Review
meta.subtitle: Harry Styles (2026)
meta.author: Pia San Miguel

body: Kiss All The Time, Disco Occasionally is proof that good things come to those who wait. Styles' much anticipated fourth studio album with a mouthful of a title is a product of a nearly 3-year hiatus from live performances, after a 22-month tour between 2021 to 2023. Notably, in a discussion with Apple Music host Zane Lowe, he detailed his experience in writing and producing the 12-track work as a personal statement of letting go of the traditional notions of brilliance and figuring out what he personally thinks is good. Following his practically nonstop public life since his rise to fame at 16 years old as a member of the teenage sensation One Direction, he now struggles with answering "Do I do this because this is what I've been doing for a long time or do I do this because I really love it?"

The album reflects a much needed journey of self-reflection and learning to establish personal boundaries as a public figure. It highlights Styles reaching out at a low point in his life to learning how to enjoy the struggle. His craft seeps into every lyric and musical arrangement, with the goal of creating something that he himself would be eager to experience in all its forms, but most particularly on the dance floor.

sections.heading: Aperture
body: While the lead single serves as the opening track, Styles recalled it actually being written towards the end of production, providing the cherry on top to the album. With themes of introspection and self-acknowledgement being part and parcel to the tracklist, Aperture revolves around the idea of holding oneself accountable for wrongdoings and the freedom that comes with that acknowledgement. To quote Styles, "...you can move forward when you acknowledge the things that you don't know and therefore give yourself the space to like let light come in."

Upon its release, its psychedelic, electronic, house-influenced nature signaled to longtime fans, such as myself, that a new chapter has commenced for the musician, whose previous music typically falls under the standard pop genre with a soft rock undertone. Its steady buildup leads listeners with ease in getting into the zone, making it nearly impossible to stay still as it plays. The addition of a call-and-response style of vocals done by a Gospel-like choir tastefully aligns with the proclamation "We belong together," hinting at the best way to listen to the song: surrounded by a high-spirited crowd in a club atmosphere, as inspired by how the song was written after a night in Berghain, Berlin's exclusive nightclub.

sections.heading: American Girls
body: A piano-driven introduction sets the scene for a moment to ponder on what makes a risk worth taking, specifically what it means to choose someone to spend your life with forever. Styles sings "Right at home with perfect timing / A face that knows her perfect lighting / 'Cause time will show that you should try it / Those American girls you spend your life with" as a response to several of his friends getting married. As the song picks up the pace from the first chorus onwards, a sense of excitement and sudden urge takes over to go after what you want in life, especially when it feels as if everyone around you has begun to settle down, while you're just getting started. American Girls captures exhilarating and nerve-wracking feelings all at once, as evidenced by Styles' powerful, exclamation-like vocals.

sections.heading: Ready, Steady, Go!
body: Keeping up with the liveliness of the first two tracks, Ready, Steady, Go! maintains a danceable beat, but with more stressful instrumentals and lyrics, suggestive of a shift in tone from thrilling to pensive. The gradual addition of drums and electrifying beats creates an undeniable tension that links to the feeling of wanting to pour all of yourself into something, be it romantically, career-related, or personally, but ultimately being held back by self-doubt. The repeated "Ready, steady, go"s are symbolic of the act of mentally preparing yourself. Approaching the song's outro, we hear Styles' screaming vocals in the background, a kind of expression familiar to all of us grappling with moving past our inner monologues.

sections.heading: Are You Listening Yet?
body: This powerhouse of a track portrays a stream of consciousness style of writing, a peek into Styles' thoughts with the experience of being on tour and performing for audiences night after night. The song's chorus, heavy with overlapping vocals and intense drums, creates a feeling of no escape, as if chained by one's own thoughts. In other words, it is exactly how I would imagine it sounds like to be bombarded with cameras in your face, constantly stalked and followed around on ordinary days, and being asked much too personal questions. It is Harry's way of voicing out his unrest, especially being immersed in such a culture before even being at legal age.

sections.heading: Taste Back
body: Possibly one of my personal favorites from the album, the lighthearted and youthful tune of Taste Back serves as a much too relatable backing track to the experience of reconnecting with someone who formerly held a big part of your heart. Its irresistible opening riff is a refreshing shift from the previously heavy beats. As Styles repeatedly sings "Do you need a little love?," he highlights the inevitable: that no matter what point in life we find ourselves in, a constant necessity is the love we seek in others.

sections.heading: The Waiting Game
body: The opening verse follows the lines "You can romanticize your shortcomings / Ignore your agency to stop / Write a ballad with the details / While skimming off the top," visualizing the cycle that Styles often falls into when it comes to making music: "write songs and get rewarded for it." The flow of the cellos create a feeling of being on a merry-go-round with no definite end. It cleverly spotlights the reality that comes with being self-aware but with a lack of motivation to do anything to address the behaviors that put you in the cycle in the first place.

sections.heading: Season 2 Weight Loss
body: While this piece specifically took me much longer to come around to, particularly because of an overwhelming amount of melodies combined into four minutes, it has now grown to be one of the tracks I have formed an attachment to since its release. Its persistent use of minor chords evokes feelings of unease and confusion, as Styles seeks validation internally and externally through the question "Do you love me now? Do I let you down?" This can serve as a response to fans, asking if his career break led to any noticeable changes. As someone who cannot bear to hear about anyone having a negative perception of me, I feel the lyricism of this song on a personal level, and I'm sure my fellow people pleasers do too.

sections.heading: Coming Up Roses
body: It is no surprise that the only track where Styles is given full credit for both the music and the lyrics is what I consider to be the star of the album. From beginning to end, he, alongside a 39-piece orchestra, takes you on an emotional journey through a love story with the highest of highs and the lowest of lows, in the form of a tear-jerking ballad. According to Styles himself, the song is about "how special something can be and not everything has to last forever in order for it to be special." With the song starting with the mere plucking of violins to a full on ensemble piece, this is representative of the evolution of a relationship from the honeymoon phase to the dreaded reality of it all, yet there exists a balance between intensity and purity, with his "la-la-la"s providing a devastatingly beautiful ending to this masterclass in songwriting.

sections.heading: Pop
body: A dramatic change from slow to upbeat comes from Pop, with striking rhythms from bass and percussion. The track starts off slow and ascends into slight chaos and instability, with Styles' elevated vocal dynamics and pitch and a dizzying synthesizer melody. As he sings "I wanted to behave, but I know I'll do it again," he portrays the difficulty in maintaining a tame, well-liked persona for fans and the public eye, while actively engaging in scandalous habits that eventually catch up to him. In relation to The Waiting Game, this song also highlights a cyclical behavior, going back to the things that provide him an escape from his celebrity life while trying not to distort the image that his tiger fanbase has of him.

sections.heading: Dance No More
body: We see the album name come to life with Dance No More's addicting disco beat, carried by a prominent bass line, playful keyboards, and use of voice distortion. The carefree and fun experience of being in the studio producing this track is evident through the call-and-response element implemented into the chorus, creating the feeling of the song best played with a crowd. The lyric "There's no difference between the tears and the sweat" provides insight into the emotion that comes with dancing your heart out and being free to express whatever may come over you.

sections.heading: Paint By Numbers
body: Styles recounted the initial desire to have this as the opening track to the album, greeting listeners with the line "Oh what a gift it is to be noticed / But it's nothing to do with me" after being away from the stage. The slow tempo and simple instrumentals mirror his desire to create something "beautifully ordinary" for himself. He strips down to the basics with the use of an acoustic guitar and the occasional French horn, intentionally done to paint himself and his story in an ordinary light.

sections.heading: Carla's Song
body: As if the album could not get any better, I would personally like to thank Carla for the life-changing creation that is this song — what I believe to be the perfect ending to this whirlwind of an album. It simply gives listeners that zest for life to experience everything to the fullest. The drums take center stage for this track. The power that it takes to wield that much energy, as captured by the bridge, requires a sense of passion like no other. For Styles, this was one of the most important parts of making this album, as it encapsulates the experience of making music: "Making music is just adding something to this world of things that is waiting for people to discover." He expresses a sense of peace and satisfaction, knowing that life will always throw something new at us to discover. For me, I find that peace and satisfaction in the security that his extensive oeuvre will live on forever, and I "can hear it anytime."

To sum it up, Kiss All The Time. Disco, Occasionally is Harry Styles at his most free and vulnerable yet. While some may interpret this differently, due to the process of a fusion of horizons, I believe that this new era, perhaps a cultural reset even, is a love letter from Styles to his younger self, telling him that he now feels less restricted and can confidently pursue his dreams while still staying authentic. Dare I say, one of the greatest comebacks in modern music history.

references.citation: Apple Music. (2026, March 5). Harry Styles - Zane Lowe & Apple Music 'Kiss All the Time. Disco, Occasionally' interview [Video]. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oTA637pJ63Y&t=9s

Ruiz, M. (2026, March 6). A totally biased, song-by-song review of Harry Styles's Kiss All the Time. Disco, Occasionally. Vogue. https://www.vogue.com/article/song-by-song-review-of-kiss-all-the-time-disco-occasionally

Levy, J. (2026, March 3). Harry Styles is up for anything on 'Kiss All The Time. Disco, Occasionally.' Rolling Stone. https://www.rollingstone.com/music/music-album-reviews/harry-styles-kiss-all-the-time-disco-occasionally-review-1235524301/
